text: {"claims":[{"statement":"Hoax-related conspiracy theories have been shown to predict refusal to engage in preventive behaviors, including vaccination.","claimType":"empirical","quote":"Hoax-related conspiracy theories have been shown to predict refusal to engage in preventive behaviors, including vaccination [25].","entailment":"The quoted sentence directly states that hoax-related conspiracy theories predict refusal to engage in preventive behaviors such as vaccination, supporting the claim.","population":"","intervention":"hoax-related conspiracy theories","comparator":"","outcome":"refusal to engage in preventive behaviors, including vaccination","timeframe":"","quantifiers":"","modality":"associational"},{"statement":"The Igbos from Southeast Nigeria were perceived to have the highest level of skepticism about COVID-19.","claimType":"empirical","quote":"A striking finding from this study is that the Igbos from Southeast Nigeria were perceived to have the highest level of skepticism about COVID-19.","entailment":"The quoted sentence reports that Igbos in Southeast Nigeria were seen as having the greatest COVID‑19 skepticism, which is the claim being made.","population":"Igbos from Southeast Nigeria","intervention":"","comparator":"","outcome":"level of skepticism about COVID-19","timeframe":"","quantifiers":"","modality":"descriptive"},{"statement":"National surveys have reported that the lowest rates of intention to receive the COVID-19 vaccine were among Southeast participants compared to those from other zones in the country.","claimType":"empirical","quote":"National surveys have reported that the lowest rates of intention to receive the COVID-19 vaccine were among Southeast participants compared to those from other zones in the country [27], [28].","entailment":"The quoted sentence states that national surveys found the lowest vaccine‑intention rates among Southeast participants versus other zones, directly supporting the claim.","population":"Southeast participants in Nigeria","intervention":"","comparator":"participants from other zones in the country","outcome":"intention to receive the COVID-19 vaccine","timeframe":"","quantifiers":"lowest rates","modality":"descriptive"},{"statement":"Sensitizing and engaging religious leaders increased the acceptance and uptake of novel health interventions in Nigeria.","claimType":"empirical","quote":"Studies have shown that sensitizing and engaging religious leaders increased the acceptance and uptake of novel health interventions in Nigeria [31].","entailment":"The quoted sentence reports that studies found that when religious leaders were sensitized and engaged, acceptance and uptake of new health interventions rose in Nigeria, which matches the claim.","population":"Nigeria","intervention":"sensitizing and engaging religious leaders","comparator":"","outcome":"acceptance and uptake of novel health interventions","timeframe":"","quantifiers":"","modality":"causal"},{"statement":"Information from public health agencies and experts such as WHO, NCDC, and NPHCDA were seen by our participants as trusted.","claimType":"empirical","quote":"It is noteworthy that information from public health agencies and experts such as WHO, NCDC, and NPHCDA were seen by our participants as trusted.","entailment":"The quoted sentence indicates that participants regarded information from those agencies as trustworthy, directly supporting the claim.","population":"participants in the study","intervention":"information from WHO, NCDC, and NPHCDA","comparator":"","outcome":"perceived trustworthiness","timeframe":"","quantifiers":"","modality":"descriptive"},{"statement":"Obtaining COVID-19-related information through traditional media sources is associated with lower beliefs in conspiracy theories and misinformation narratives.","claimType":"empirical","quote":"We found a high level of trust in information from radio and television among community members. This finding is similar to other studies that reported that obtaining COVID-19-related information through traditional media sources is associated with lower beliefs in conspiracy theories and misinformation narratives [22], [41], [43].","entailment":"The quoted passage cites studies showing an association between using traditional media for COVID‑19 information and reduced belief in conspiracy theories, supporting the claim.","population":"community members","intervention":"obtaining COVID-19 information through traditional media sources (radio, television)","comparator":"","outcome":"lower beliefs in conspiracy theories and misinformation narratives","timeframe":"","quantifiers":"","modality":"associational"},{"statement":"Exposure to digital media was associated with greater belief in conspiracy theories and misinformation.","claimType":"empirical","quote":"Conversely, exposure to digital media was associated with greater belief in conspiracy theories and misinformation [41], [44], [45].","entailment":"The quoted sentence directly states that digital media exposure correlates with higher belief in conspiracy theories and misinformation, which underpins the claim.","population":"","intervention":"exposure to digital media","comparator":"","outcome":"greater belief in conspiracy theories and misinformation","timeframe":"","quantifiers":"","modality":"associational"}]}
